subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Tidal Forces and Spaghettification
description: A 60-second lesson on Tidal Forces and Spaghettification.

hook.text: Near a black hole, gravity pulls harder on your near side than your far side. The result: you get stretched like spaghetti.

buildup.text: Tidal forces increase as you approach. At a stellar-mass black hole, they become lethal well before you reach the event horizon.

discovery.text: In 2020, astronomers watched a star get spaghettified by a black hole in real time—ripped apart in a tidal disruption event.

twist.text: Supermassive black holes are different—you'd cross the horizon alive. It's so far from the center that tidal forces stay gentle.

climax.text: Tidal disruption events produce brilliant flares visible billions of light-years away as the star's material spirals inward.

punchline.text: Stretched into cosmic spaghetti. Gravity's most dramatic display of uneven pull.

quiz.question: Why would you survive crossing a supermassive black hole's event horizon but not a stellar-mass one?
options: The event horizon is so far from the center that tidal forces are gentler there
Supermassive black holes have weaker gravity overall than stellar-mass ones
The event horizon of a supermassive black hole moves slower, reducing the stretching
